eyebrow: Territory Palette · Sourced from real places
tagline: Forty-eight territories, read for their colour, not their postcard.
metaNote:
Color sourced from material
culture, light & pigment tradition
sourcing: Every palette is sampled from a real place's material culture, its specific light condition, and the historical pigment tradition available there — the colour you would actually see standing in the territory, in its working season, not the saturated idea the postcard sells.

volumes.title: Founding Four
volumes.eyebrow: Field Notebook № 04 · Travel · Generative Palette Study
preface: The category defaults are rejected as primary signal: Santorini blue, resort turquoise, safari ochre, foliage red. They are not wrong — they are unearned without specific cultural grounding.

Each palette is sourced from three places only — the territory's material culture, its specific light condition, and the historical pigment tradition available to the people of the place. The test is recognition: someone who has been here should know the palette without seeing the label.

palettes.kicker: 59° N · January · 14:00 · Lake Baikal corridor
palettes.title: A condensation window on the № 002, somewhere between Krasnoyarsk and Irkutsk
palettes.source: Color drawn from the narrow palette of Russian rail interiors and the silver-on-silver light of taiga at midwinter: tarnished samovar brass in the restaurant car, the velour upholstery of second-class kupé, birch bark beyond the glass, and the brief violet of 3pm dusk at this latitude. The dominant note is not blue; it is the cool silver of frozen birch forest under overcast.

swatches.name: Frozen birch forest at noon, silver
swatches.note: Snow-laden taiga under overcast at 59° N; the dominant reading of the entire trip from the train window.

swatches.name: Window condensation, compartment side
swatches.note: Glass fogged by breath, warmed faintly by the yellow heater bulb at the carriage end.

swatches.name: Birch bark, peeling, betula pendula
swatches.note: Chalky surface of the dominant taiga species, slightly warm against the snow.

swatches.name: Samovar brass, lacquer scoured off
swatches.note: Restaurant-car samovar, the lacquer worn through by decades of provodnitsa hands.

swatches.name: Kupé upholstery, deep maroon velour
swatches.note: The carriage-interior fabric standardised across RZhD wagons since the late Soviet period.

swatches.name: Three-o'clock dusk, ultramarine over snow
swatches.note: Civil twilight comes early at this latitude in January; the snow takes the sky's shadow before the lamps come on.

d.text: Birch-forest silver anchors every surface; nothing competes with it.

s.text: Condensation, birch bark, samovar brass — the interior warmth that keeps the silver from being clinical.

a.text: Maroon and dusk-violet only in small quantity, as punctuation.

palettes.refuses: Saturated Soviet-flag red. The parade-square cliché flattens any Russia palette into iconography; this trip is the carriage interior, not the May Day square.

palettes.kicker: 31° N · April · 18:45 · Souk Semmarine, Marrakech medina
palettes.title: The fifteen minutes when the souk's roof slats stop letting in white light and start letting in blue
palettes.source: Color drawn from the pigment culture of Marrakech: tadelakt lime-plaster walls warmed by a setting sun, saffron and cumin in burlap mounds, pierced-brass lanterns beginning to be lit. The cobalt is the strip of sky read through a roof gap; the Majorelle is named as a 1924 colonial import, not as native to the territory. The dominant is the rose of plaster, not the postcard blue.

swatches.name: Tadelakt rose, late sun on plaster
swatches.note: Lime-and-marble plaster of the medina, polished with olive-soap stone; the colour of the souk walls at golden hour.

swatches.name: Zellige white, tea-stained
swatches.note: Hand-cut glazed mosaic tile, the ground colour between the geometric darks.

swatches.name: Saffron, oxidised in burlap
swatches.note: Cured threads from the Atlas highlands; mound colour rather than fresh stigma.

swatches.name: Lantern through pierced brass
swatches.note: Light cast on the floor through a hanging Marrakchi lantern as the day shifts to lamp-light.

swatches.name: Cobalt sky through the roof gap
swatches.note: A narrow strip of post-sunset sky read between palm-frond slats overhead.

swatches.name: Majorelle, named as colonial import (1924)
swatches.note: Synthetic ultramarine popularised by Jacques Majorelle; included as accent, not as identity, with its origin named.

d.text: Tadelakt rose carries the palette; warm plaster reads from across a square.

s.text: Saffron, brass-light, zellige white — the warm range of the souk floor and goods.

a.text: The two blues only as small jolts — the sky-gap and the Majorelle quotation.

palettes.refuses: Mediterranean-postcard turquoise. That blue belongs to noon at Chefchaouen, not to dusk in the Marrakech medina; using it here would smear two distinct Moroccos together for tourist legibility.

palettes.kicker: 23° S · December · 16:20 · Salar de Atacama, 2,305 m
palettes.title: Late afternoon on the salt flat, with the Licancabur shadow already starting to walk east
palettes.source: Color from a place where almost nothing grows: halite crust bleached white, iron-oxide rust of Andean rock, the specific cold violet of high-altitude shadow on pale ground, undyed vicuña wool from the altiplano herds. The blue is the bleached cobalt of thin-atmosphere sky at 2,300 m, never saturated. The palette is mineral before it is anything else.

swatches.name: Halite crust, sun-bleached
swatches.note: Sodium-chloride crust at the salar's edge, faintly warmed by sub-pixel mineral dust.

swatches.name: Vicuña wool, undyed, altiplano herd
swatches.note: Natural fibre tone of vicugna vicugna, the textile of Aymara and Atacameño weaving traditions.

swatches.name: Andean copper terracotta
swatches.note: Iron-oxide rust of the Cordillera de la Sal and the adobe walls of San Pedro.

swatches.name: Volcanic basalt, weathered
swatches.note: Andesitic scree on the Licancabur flanks; the dark mineral that grounds the white.

swatches.name: High-altitude shadow, lilac on pale ground
swatches.note: The specific cold violet that appears in shadows at 2,300 m when there is almost no atmospheric scatter; only visible against white salt.

swatches.name: Atacama sky, bleached cobalt
swatches.note: Thin atmosphere and extreme UV produce a blue with less depth than at sea level — almost dusty.

d.text: Halite white anchors the salar; everything else is read against it.

s.text: Vicuña, copper terracotta, basalt — the warm earths that make the white feel like ground, not paper.

a.text: Shadow lilac and bleached cobalt only at small scale, where the altitude actually shows.

palettes.refuses: Any green. Nothing chlorophyllous grows on the salar; the smallest tuft of cactus-green would betray the territory and turn it into a generic desert.

palettes.kicker: 37° N · November · 05:40 · MV passing Kea, en route Piraeus
palettes.title: The fluorescent-lit passenger lounge of an off-season ferry, an hour before docking
palettes.source: Color from a working ship, not a postcard: grey-green Aegean swell with no plankton bloom, weathered fibreglass hull paint, diesel sheen on the water along the rail, a single overhead T8 fluorescent tube in the saloon, the funnel stripe sun-faded almost to grey. The horizon is barely distinguishable from the water. This is the Greek islands as the people who actually use the ferries see them.

swatches.name: November Aegean swell, grey-green
swatches.note: Aegean water in late autumn: no algal bloom, no resort blue, churned grey-green under overcast.

swatches.name: Weathered fibreglass, hull paint
swatches.note: GRP marine paint, yellowed by years of salt and sun; the off-white of every working hull.

swatches.name: Horizon, indeterminate
swatches.note: The grey where sky meets sea; low contrast, no line you could draw.

swatches.name: Funnel stripe, faded indigo enamel
swatches.note: Company colour on the smokestack, alkyd marine enamel, sun-bleached down a register or two.

swatches.name: Diesel sheen on the water, near the rail
swatches.note: Iridescent dark grey-purple film along the waterline below the gangway.

swatches.name: Fluorescent T8, passenger lounge
swatches.note: Single overhead tube in the saloon; the slightly sickly green-white that makes everyone's face look like they didn't sleep.

d.text: Aegean grey-green; the colour you spend the night staring at through the window.

s.text: Weathered fibreglass, horizon grey, faded indigo — the ship's own muted vocabulary.

a.text: Diesel and fluorescent only as flicker; introduce them anywhere larger and the palette stops being honest.

palettes.refuses: Cycladic cerulean and bleach-bone white. That palette is high summer at Oia; this is November at five a.m. on a working route — the same archipelago, an unrelated colour world.

volumes.title: Tokyo · Lisbon · Iceland · Kerala
volumes.eyebrow: Field Notebook № 04 · Travel · Vol II · Generative Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four more territories, further from the postcard.
preface: This volume pushes harder against the category default. Tokyo is not sakura pink; Lisbon is not azulejo blue as primary; Iceland is not aurora green; Kerala is not backwater turquoise. Each is a specific hour and a specific surface — read the kicker before the swatches.
As before: every swatch names its material, pigment, or light source. The hierarchy is enforced. The refuses line is part of the spec, not editorial flavour.

palettes.kicker: 35° N · February · 23:48 · Yamanote line, last loop, between Shinjuku and Ikebukuro
palettes.title: The last train of the night, fluorescent-lit, with the overcoat-thick warmth of a hundred salarymen leaning on the windows
palettes.source: Color from a Japanese commuter rail car after midnight in winter: cool fluorescent saloon white, the navy wool of suit overcoats, JR East moquette upholstery, the Shōwa-era cream tile of the platform walls. The accents are the wet-pavement spill of side-street neon and the faded red of a vending machine in the underpass.

swatches.name: Fluorescent saloon white, mid-car
swatches.note: Cool overhead lighting in a JR East 235-series car, reading slightly blue against the platform glow.

swatches.name: Shōwa-era platform tile, cream
swatches.note: Glazed wall tile of older Yamanote stations; the cream that pre-dates the LED-white refurbishment.

swatches.name: Salaryman wool, navy at 30°
swatches.note: The standard winter suit cloth; the dominant moving colour, not the dominant surface colour.

swatches.name: Moquette plum, JR East seat
swatches.note: The patterned wool-blend upholstery on the bench seats; designed to hide the seventeen-year wear pattern.

swatches.name: Sodium-pink neon, wet pavement
swatches.note: Reflection of a side-street sign in a Kabukichō puddle as the train pulls past; only visible at this hour, in this weather.

swatches.name: Vending machine red, faded
swatches.note: The corporate red of an old Coca-Cola / Suntory machine in the station underpass, sun-bleached two registers down.

d.text: Saloon white reads from every flat surface inside the car.

s.text: Platform cream, salaryman navy, moquette plum — the resting surfaces of the system.

a.text: Neon-pink and faded red only as small punctuation through windows.

palettes.refuses: Cherry-blossom pink. Sakura belongs to a Hanami picnic in April; using it in a Tokyo palette without naming the hour collapses the city into a tourism poster.

palettes.kicker: 38° N · May · 11:20 · Tram 28 ascending to Largo da Graça
palettes.title: The narrow climb past the cathedral, where the calçada is half limestone, half basalt, and the tram livery has fifty years of sun on it
palettes.source: Color from the working tram and the streets it climbs: calçada portuguesa in its two-stone pattern, the sun-faded ochre of an old Carris carriage, building paint dulled toward pink, raw cork from the Alentejo. The blue appears only on the tile; the water is not in this palette.

swatches.name: Calçada limestone, sun-polished
swatches.note: Pale Estremadura limestone, hand-cut to a 4cm cube; the dominant ground texture of the city.

swatches.name: Calçada basalt, interleaved seam
swatches.note: Dark basalt set in patterns into the limestone field — the half of the pavement that makes the other half legible.

swatches.name: Faded paint-on-stucco, Largo das Portas
swatches.note: Building paint near Portas do Sol, originally red oxide, weathered to a dusty pink across one Atlantic decade.

swatches.name: Cork bark, raw, Alentejo
swatches.note: Stripped Quercus suber bark from the cork plantations south of the Tagus; the material culture you can buy in every souvenir shop, but here as bark, not as gimmick.

swatches.name: Azulejo blue, Pombaline tin-glaze
swatches.note: Cobalt-on-tin glaze, 18th-c. Lisboa tradition; on facades only, never extended into the dominant.

swatches.name: Tram livery yellow, oxidised
swatches.note: The Carris yellow as it appears on the actual 28 — not on the brochure version — after fifty Lisbon summers.

d.text: Limestone calçada carries every street and square the tram passes.

s.text: Basalt seam, faded pink, cork — the working materials of the city, not its tourist signage.

a.text: Azulejo blue and tram yellow only where they actually exist: on tile and on livery.

palettes.refuses: Azulejo cobalt as primary. Lisbon as "the blue city" is a postcard premise; the Atlantic light here is cooler and the dominant surface is stone, not tile.

palettes.kicker: 63° N · Late August · 15:00 · Reynisfjara, south coast of Iceland
palettes.title: The black-sand beach below the basalt cliff, with the glacial-melt river-mouth still running silt-blue twenty metres offshore
palettes.source: Color from a volcanic shore at the end of summer: basalt sand, the milky blue of glacial outwash, undyed Icelandic sheep wool, wind-scoured tussock grass, and late-season invasive lupine gone past its peak. Almost no warm colour. The sky is rarely a contributor here.

swatches.name: Basalt sand, Reynisfjara
swatches.note: Sub-millimetre fragments of erupted basalt; the only beach surface that reads black even when wet.

swatches.name: Sheep wool, raw and unscoured
swatches.note: Icelandic sheep fleece before washing or dyeing — the lanolin-cream of an unspun roving.

swatches.name: Glacial-melt silt, river-mouth blue
swatches.note: Suspended rock flour from a sandur outwash plain; the colour of an Icelandic river where it meets the sea.

swatches.name: Tussock grass, wind-scoured
swatches.note: Hardy Festuca on the cliff above the beach; the only chlorophyll in the territory and only at small scale.

swatches.name: Lupine, past its peak
swatches.note: Invasive Lupinus nootkatensis, introduced for soil reclamation; brilliant in June, this purple-grey by late August.

swatches.name: Basalt column, wet sea-stack
swatches.note: Hexagonal columnar basalt at the Reynisdrangar stacks; the same mineral as the sand, with a cooler cast when soaked.

d.text: Basalt sand — the territory's only honest "neutral."

s.text: Wool cream, glacial silt, tussock grass — the materials that keep the palette from going to charcoal.

a.text: Late-summer lupine and wet basalt, in small reads only.

palettes.refuses: Aurora green. The northern lights are a winter image and a tourist-board cliché — this is August midday at 63° N, when the sky is overcast and the sun never sets enough to see them.

palettes.kicker: 10° N · August · 16:00 · Fort Kochi, the hour between two squalls
palettes.title: Wet laterite walls and Portuguese-import indigo trim, the banyan still dripping, the brass of the temple lamp polished by rain
palettes.source: Color from a colonial port in deep monsoon: exposed laterite rock on every wall and roof, Portuguese-import indigo trim from four hundred years of trading-post construction, banyan leaf still wet, coir rope soaked through, brass diya oil lamps. The sky is slate; the sea is slate; the colour is on the buildings.

swatches.name: Wet laterite, exposed wall
swatches.note: Iron-rich laterite rock cut into blocks and tile; reads dark and saturated after rain, lighter after the sun returns.

swatches.name: Banyan leaf, post-rain
swatches.note: Broad Ficus benghalensis foliage, still dripping; deepest green that survives the overcast.

swatches.name: Rain-light, between squalls
swatches.note: The cool grey-blue of the Arabian Sea sky in monsoon; this is what the Kerala blue actually looks like, not turquoise.

swatches.name: Coconut coir, soaked rope
swatches.note: Hand-spun coir from the Alappuzha coast; dark brown when wet, warmer when dry.

swatches.name: Portuguese-import indigo, trim
swatches.note: Indigo trim on shutters and door frames, surviving from 16th-c. Portuguese paint stocks — cited here as colonial import, like Majorelle in Vol I.

swatches.name: Brass diya, polished by rain
swatches.note: Hindu temple-lamp brass at a wayside shrine; the only warm metal in the territory.

d.text: Laterite red is the colour of every wall and roof tile; it carries the territory.

s.text: Wet banyan, rain-light, coir — the working textures of the port.

a.text: Indigo and brass only where colonial trade or temple use put them.

palettes.refuses: Tropical-postcard turquoise. The Arabian Sea in monsoon is the colour of slate — using lagoon-blue here misfiles Kerala as the Maldives.

volumes.title: Faroes · Mongolia · Vienna · New Mexico
volumes.eyebrow: Field Notebook № 04 · Travel · Vol III · Generative Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four more interiors, mostly at night or under weather.
preface: Volume III leans inward. Three of the four territories are interiors — a felt-walled ger, a Viennese kaffeehaus, an adobe house seen between mountain stops — and the fourth is a Faroese harbour so thickly fogged that it reads as an interior anyway. Light here is small, local, and almost always from below or behind.
As before: every swatch names its source. The hierarchy is enforced. The refuses line names the colour that would betray the territory.

palettes.kicker: 62° N · September · 09:30 · Tórshavn waterfront, thick sea-fog
palettes.title: A Faroese harbour at half-visibility, where the painted-house pigments are the only saturated colours in fifty metres
palettes.source: Color from the North Atlantic in fog: the diffuse grey of sea-mist, tarred-wood cladding on the old harbour buildings, the two paint pigments the Faroese actually maintain — Sjóvinnubankin ochre and turf-roof oxblood, undyed wool and wet peat. There is no real blue in this weather.

swatches.name: North Atlantic sea-fog, mid-morning
swatches.note: The almost-achromatic grey of a true Faroese mist; reads slightly green only against the painted houses.

swatches.name: Tarred wood, old Tinganes cladding
swatches.note: Pitch-coated timber on the harbour's oldest buildings; near-black, with a warm undertone.

swatches.name: Wool oil cream, raw fleece
swatches.note: Undyed Føroyskt sheep wool with lanolin still in it; the cream of a fisherman's jumper before it is washed.

swatches.name: Wet peat, field edge
swatches.note: Cut turf for fuel and roofing; the dark organic ground that surrounds every Faroese settlement.

swatches.name: Painted-house ochre, Sjóvinnubankin
swatches.note: Saturated yellow-ochre on Tinganes — one of the two paint pigments the Faroese consistently maintain.

swatches.name: Painted-house oxblood, turf-roof saltbox
swatches.note: Iron-oxide red on the turf-roofed houses; the only other saturated pigment the climate tolerates.

d.text: Fog grey reads from sky, sea, and stone all at once.

s.text: Tarred wood, wool, peat — the structural and material darks.

a.text: Ochre and oxblood — the painted houses are the entire colour event.

palettes.refuses: Any clean blue. Faroese sea-fog has almost no blue in it; introducing one would lie about the visibility and about what the Atlantic actually does to colour up here.

palettes.kicker: 47° N · October · 05:55 · Ger camp at Övörkhangai, the moment before sunrise
palettes.title: Inside the felt, with the dung-fire still burning and the door-flap open to the cold blue civil-twilight
palettes.source: Color from a Mongolian ger on the steppe at the end of the warm season: undyed sheep felt on every interior surface, the warm reflection of a dung-fire on the inside wall, the cold blue of civil twilight through the open flap, khadag silk in Tengri blue tied to the door pole, horsehair, wind-borne dust.

swatches.name: Sheep felt, undyed
swatches.note: The felted-wool covering of the ger, inside and out; the dominant surface of the entire encampment.

swatches.name: Steppe dust, dawn
swatches.note: Wind-borne loess that coats every flat surface in the camp by morning.

swatches.name: Horsehair, bay
swatches.note: Mane and tail rope used for halter, reins, and the small wall ornaments inside the ger.

swatches.name: Civil twilight, cold blue
swatches.note: The narrow band of light through the open door-flap at 5:55 am, 47° N, October — before the sun is up but after the stars are gone.

swatches.name: Dung-fire on felt, interior wall
swatches.note: Argal (dried-dung) flame reflected onto the inside felt; warm orange, never red.

swatches.name: Khadag silk, Tengri blue
swatches.note: Ceremonial blue scarf tied to the support pole or the head of the herd; the single saturated colour in the ger.

d.text: Felt is the territory; every other reading is read against it.

s.text: Steppe dust, horsehair, dawn blue — the working textures of the camp.

a.text: Hearth-fire and khadag blue, in tiny quantities.

palettes.refuses: Saturated grassland green. The "endless green steppe" image belongs to June; in October the steppe is straw, dust, and felt — using a green primary here puts you in the wrong month.

palettes.kicker: 48° N · November · 18:50 · A wet evening in a Viennese kaffeehaus, Mariahilf
palettes.title: The working café, not the imperial pageant: marble table, banquette leather, a century of tobacco on the plaster
palettes.source: Color from a kaffeehaus that has not been refurbished: the warm ivory of nicotine-aged plaster overhead, dark banquette leather in the Thonet-era olive that survives in older rooms, oxidised brass hat-racks, the grey-veined marble table top, gilt mirror lettering. The accent is the wet cobble visible through the window.

swatches.name: Nicotine-aged plaster, ceiling
swatches.note: A century of tobacco on the plaster overhead, never washed; the warm ivory that defines a real kaffeehaus interior.

swatches.name: Marble table top, grey-veined
swatches.note: Carrara-style table marble, cool against the warm plaster above it.

swatches.name: Banquette leather, Thonet olive
swatches.note: Dark olive-green dyed leather on the wall-side banquette; original to several Mariahilf-quarter cafés.

swatches.name: Oxidised brass, hat-rack and rail
swatches.note: Patinated brass fittings on every coat hook and brass rail; warm but not gold.

swatches.name: Gilt mirror lettering
swatches.note: Gold-leaf signage on the back-bar mirror; the single bright thing in the room when the lamps are low.

swatches.name: Wet cobble, through the window
swatches.note: Mariahilfer Straße at dusk after rain, glimpsed past the curtain — almost-black, very slightly warm.

d.text: Tobacco-aged plaster — the literal atmosphere of the room.

s.text: Marble, banquette green, oxidised brass — the furniture and fittings.

a.text: Gilt and wet cobble only as flicker.

palettes.refuses: Imperial red. Habsburg-pageant red turns a working café into a tourist set; this kaffeehaus is the one with the regulars and the chess clock, not the one with the souvenir mug.

palettes.kicker: 36° N · February · 16:30 · The high-desert road between Taos and Chama
palettes.title: Late winter on the high desert, with adobe still holding the day's heat and the snow on the north slope already gone blue
palettes.source: Color from northern New Mexico in late February: adobe walls of Pueblo Revival houses warmed by the low sun, sage across the valley floor, Spanish-colonial blue trim on door and window frames (a regional protective pigment, not decorative), juniper bark, and the cold blue of shaded snow on the Sangre de Cristos.

swatches.name: Adobe wall, low-angle sun
swatches.note: Sun-warmed Pueblo Revival earth wall, read on the south face at 16:30 in February.

swatches.name: Sage, mountain mahogany
swatches.note: Artemisia tridentata at the valley floor; the only consistent green in the territory.

swatches.name: Shaded-snow blue, Sangre de Cristos
swatches.note: Late-winter snow on the north slope, already in shadow at this hour; the cold-blue reading that makes the adobe look warm.

swatches.name: Juniper bark, twisted
swatches.note: Juniperus monosperma on the road verge; the dark warm that anchors sage and adobe together.

swatches.name: Spanish-colonial blue, door trim
swatches.note: Indigo trim around windows and doors — a protective pigment in Spanish-colonial folk tradition, not decoration.

swatches.name: Alpenglow on snow, peach
swatches.note: Late-winter alpenglow on the high snowfield, only available in the last twenty minutes before the sun drops behind the range.

d.text: Adobe carries the territory; every wall, every road shoulder.

s.text: Sage, juniper bark, shaded-snow blue — the land's other constants.

a.text: Door-trim blue and alpenglow peach only where they actually appear.

palettes.refuses: Tourist-shop "Southwestern turquoise" as primary. The stone is real; the palette is not. Anchoring on it makes the territory legible only via the souvenir.

volumes.title: Coastal & Maritime
volumes.eyebrow: Field Notebook № 04 · Travel · Vol IV · Generative Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four shorelines, four hours of working light.
preface: Coastal palettes are the most pre-conquered category in the colour world: resort turquoise, postcard cobalt, white-sand cream. Every territory in this volume refuses one of those defaults outright, because the real working coast — a Newfoundland outport, a Mumbai koliwada, a Breton quay, a Hokkaido kelp shore — does not look like the brochure.
Each palette names its hour, its weather, and the material being worked. Primary by surface area, secondary by frequency, accent by punctuation. The refuses line names the postcard.

palettes.kicker: 47° N · June · 10:00 · St. John's harbour, dense Atlantic fog
palettes.title: A Newfoundland outport with the saltbox-red clapboard reading at twice its hue against the white
palettes.source: Color drawn from a working fishing village in early-summer fog: saltbox-red clapboard, oiled dory grey, kelp-wrack at low tide, lichen on the rock shoulder. The dominant is the fog itself; the painted houses are accents because there are only three in eyesight.

swatches.name: North Atlantic fog cream
swatches.note: Low-altitude sea-mist over the harbour; the dominant reading for the first three hours of the morning.

swatches.name: Dory grey, gunwale paint
swatches.note: Sun-and-salt oil-paint on the working boats; the standard outport hull colour.

swatches.name: Atlantic harbour green
swatches.note: Tidal water inside the breakwater, dulled by suspended kelp.

swatches.name: Kelp wrack, low tide
swatches.note: Bladderwrack and rockweed exposed on the slipway stones.

swatches.name: Saltbox red, painted clapboard
swatches.note: Iron-oxide marine paint on the houses above the harbour; reads at twice its hue in fog.

swatches.name: Rock lichen, orange
swatches.note: Xanthoria lichen on the exposed granite of the headland.

d.text: Fog cream sits on the boats, the houses, the water, the sky.

s.text: Dory grey, harbour green, kelp brown — the working surfaces.

a.text: Saltbox red and lichen orange, the only saturated colours in the cove.

palettes.refuses: Postcard cobalt. The North Atlantic in June fog is grey-green; the picture-book blue is a calendar shot, not the morning.

palettes.kicker: 19° N · December · 06:20 · Worli koliwada, Mumbai, just before sunrise
palettes.title: A working koli fishing village at dawn, the painted boats still on the slipway and the nets out drying
palettes.source: Color from a Mumbai fishing community at dawn in the cool dry season: cobalt-painted hulls of the koli boats, dried Bombay-duck fish on the rack, fishwife sari indigo, monsoon-stained concrete jetty. The light is the brief warm pink of an Arabian-Sea sunrise over an industrial coast.

swatches.name: Monsoon-stained concrete, jetty
swatches.note: The dominant ground texture of the koliwada — concrete that has seen ten monsoons.

swatches.name: Painted hull cobalt, koli boat
swatches.note: The boat-paint blue maintained across the koli fleet; a working pigment, not a decorative one.

swatches.name: Fishwife sari indigo
swatches.note: Deep indigo-purple sari worn under the working drape; the dominant moving colour in the village.

swatches.name: Drying net, nylon green
swatches.note: Mass-produced fishing-mesh slung between the masts to dry overnight.

swatches.name: Bombay duck, sun-dried
swatches.note: Bombil fish on the drying rack; mottled cream-pink in the early light.

swatches.name: Arabian-Sea dawn pink
swatches.note: The fifteen-minute warm sky over a haze-loaded industrial horizon.

d.text: Concrete jetty grey carries every surface above the waterline.

s.text: Cobalt hulls, sari indigo, net green — the working colours of the koli.

a.text: Dawn-pink sky and dried-fish cream as brief flashes.

palettes.refuses: The glossy 'Incredible India' saturated yellow. This is the working koliwada, not the tourism campaign.

palettes.kicker: 48° N · February · 11:00 · Saint-Malo quay at the year's lowest tide
palettes.title: A Breton granite quay laid bare by the spring tide, with oyster baskets stacked against the slipway wall
palettes.source: Color from a Channel port at the lowest tides of February: Breton granite in its blue-grey, oyster-shell beds beyond the wall, kelp on the wet stones, slate roof tiles washed by a salt fog. The Channel is grey almost the whole year here — the postcard turquoise belongs further south.

swatches.name: Breton granite, blue-grey
swatches.note: Quarried Cap Fréhel granite cut into the seawall blocks; the city's structural colour.

swatches.name: Channel fog white
swatches.note: Mid-morning salt-mist that lifts only slowly off the bay.

swatches.name: Oyster shell, sun-bleached
swatches.note: Discarded oyster halves from the Cancale beds; a regional material-culture cream.

swatches.name: Slate roof, salt-washed
swatches.note: Cap-de-la-Hague slate on the old quayside houses, kept dark by the spray.

swatches.name: Wet rockweed brown
swatches.note: Fucus on the exposed lower stones, glossy at low tide.

swatches.name: Creosote wood, pier piling
swatches.note: Tar-coated piling visible only at the year's lowest tide.

d.text: Granite is the entire built fabric of the port.

s.text: Fog white, oyster shell, slate roof — the materials around the granite.

a.text: Rockweed and creosote, only where the tide exposes them.

palettes.refuses: Atlantic-postcard turquoise. The English Channel is grey-green almost the whole year; the turquoise is a Mediterranean lie.

palettes.kicker: 42° N · July · 06:00 · Hidaka coast, Hokkaido, low tide at the height of kombu season
palettes.title: Two hundred metres of black volcanic shore, with kombu laid out on woven mats to sun-dry until noon
palettes.source: Color from a Japanese kelp-harvest shore in mid-summer: wet kombu still in the boats, sun-dried kombu on the mats, the dark volcanic sand of the Hidaka coast, a fisherman's rubber bib, woven straw drying mats. The dominant is the cream of the mats, because there are thousands of them.

swatches.name: Drying mat, woven rice straw
swatches.note: The standard komo mat used to sun-dry kombu; covers the entire shore by mid-morning.

swatches.name: Wet kombu, blackish kelp
swatches.note: Freshly hauled Saccharina japonica, still seawater-glossy.

swatches.name: Sun-dried kombu, olive ribbon
swatches.note: The same kelp eight hours later — colour shifted from black to olive as it dries.

swatches.name: Hokkaido sea grey-green
swatches.note: Northern Pacific water at this latitude in summer; never a tropical hue.

swatches.name: Fisherman's rubber bib, faded blue
swatches.note: The standard kelp-harvest apron, sun-faded from a deeper navy.

swatches.name: Volcanic black sand
swatches.note: Hidaka shore sand, derived from regional basalt.

d.text: Straw drying mats — the entire shore from above looks like cream.

s.text: Two registers of kombu plus the cool Hokkaido sea.

a.text: Fisherman bib blue and volcanic sand only as small reads.

palettes.refuses: White-sand resort blue. The Hokkaido working coast is dark sand, cold water, and seaweed — the brochure beach is a different country, a different industry.

volumes.title: Mountain & Altitude
volumes.eyebrow: Field Notebook № 04 · Travel · Vol V · Generative Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from thinner air, three months from peak season.
preface: Altitude does things to colour: less atmosphere to scatter blue, more UV to bleach reds, ground that holds heat unevenly between sun and shadow. These four territories are sampled in the working season, not the photographer's golden hour: a Khumbu teahouse at supper, Cuzco's plaza in winter morning, a Dolomites refuge at season's end, a Ladakhi monastery at dusk.

Each one refuses an obvious tourist signifier — saturated red on the prayer flag, the woven-textile rainbow, the apres-ski neon, the saffron-robe orange — and finds the colour in the wall, the smoke, the stove, the snow.

palettes.kicker: 27° N · October · 17:30 · A teahouse in Khumbu, on the trekking route from Namche to Tengboche
palettes.title: Inside the teahouse at last light, with the yak-dung stove lit and the kitchen run by lamp
palettes.source: Color from a Sherpa teahouse at 3,400 m: yak-butter lamp light, the soot-iron of the stove, snow glare still on the walls through the small window, sun-faded prayer-flag fabric pinned above the door, the autumn rust of rhododendron leaf.

swatches.name: Snow-glare white, mountain reflection
swatches.note: Reflected light off the peaks behind the teahouse, still bright at this hour.

swatches.name: Wood-smoke veil, interior
swatches.note: The atmosphere of the room itself — every interior surface reads through this haze.

swatches.name: Stove iron, soot black
swatches.note: The yak-dung stove at the centre of the kitchen; near-black with a warm cast.

swatches.name: Prayer flag, faded blue
swatches.note: One of the five flag colours, sun-bleached past saturation; pinned above the kitchen door.

swatches.name: Yak-butter lamp, gold flame
swatches.note: Lit during prayer hours and at supper; the only warm source after sunset.

swatches.name: Rhododendron leaf, autumn rust
swatches.note: Late-October leaf colour on the slope behind the teahouse — the only deep-red of the territory.

d.text: Snow-glare white anchors the territory; nothing reads warmer than it.

s.text: Smoke, stove iron, faded prayer-flag blue — the room you sit in.

a.text: Butter-lamp gold and rhododendron rust, in small reads only.

palettes.refuses: Saturated red as primary. Red is one of the five flag colours but anchoring on it produces a Tibet-by-tourist-poster palette; the teahouse is smoke and snow first.

palettes.kicker: 13° S · June · 09:00 · Plaza de Armas, Cuzco, in dry-season winter
palettes.title: The plaza at mid-morning, with the colonial ochre walls still cold and the Inca foundation stones already warming
palettes.source: Color from the Andean capital in winter morning at 3,400 m: Inca stonework at the base of every building, Spanish-colonial ochre plaster above it, terracotta tile, regional Andean textile-red, the thin highland sky.

swatches.name: Spanish-colonial ochre plaster
swatches.note: The dominant building colour around the plaza, applied over Inca foundations.

swatches.name: Inca stone, blue-grey andesite
swatches.note: Pre-conquest masonry at the base of every wall, fitted without mortar.

swatches.name: Terracotta tile, sun-bleached
swatches.note: Roof tile across the cathedral district, bleached from a deeper red.

swatches.name: Eucalyptus, hillside green-grey
swatches.note: Imported eucalyptus on the slopes above the plaza — the dominant green of modern Cuzco.

swatches.name: Andean red, woven textile
swatches.note: Cochineal-derived red in the regional manta textiles; used here as accent, not as identity.

swatches.name: Highland sky, thin atmosphere
swatches.note: Blue at 3,400 m: less saturated than at sea level, less dense than at the salar.

d.text: Ochre plaster is the city's dominant facade colour.

s.text: Inca stone, tile, eucalyptus — the working materials of Cuzco.

a.text: Andean red and highland sky only as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Tourist-rainbow Andean-textile stripe. The weaving tradition is real; using it at high frequency turns Cuzco into a souvenir poncho.

palettes.kicker: 46° N · March · 15:00 · A refuge in the Italian Dolomites, last weekend before closure
palettes.title: The end of the ski season, when the snow is sun-cupped and the larch on the slope below is still last autumn's russet
palettes.source: Color from a Dolomites rifugio as the season ends: the famous dolomia limestone pink, larch foliage gone-over from autumn, oiled-spruce refuge interior, regimental wool blankets, glacial-slope shadow, an emptied prosecco bottle on the railing.

swatches.name: Dolomia limestone pink, sun-lit peak
swatches.note: The defining pink-tinted limestone of the Dolomites at full afternoon sun.

swatches.name: Refuge wood, oiled spruce
swatches.note: Interior cladding of the rifugio, oiled annually against the winter.

swatches.name: Mountain-shadow blue, glacial slope
swatches.note: Cold shadow on the north face directly across the valley.

swatches.name: Wool blanket, regimental grey
swatches.note: Alpini-issue wool blanket folded on every bunk.

swatches.name: Larch russet, gone over
swatches.note: Last autumn's larch needles, still on the trees, no longer bright.

swatches.name: Prosecco bottle green, emptied
swatches.note: An end-of-season relic on the railing; the only saturated green visible.

d.text: Dolomia pink reads from the peaks above the refuge all afternoon.

s.text: Refuge wood, blanket grey, mountain-shadow blue — the closed-up interior.

a.text: Larch russet and prosecco green, end-of-season punctuation.

palettes.refuses: Apres-ski fluorescent. The on-piste neon belongs to the in-season weekend, not the day the lifts go off.

palettes.kicker: 34° N · November · 17:00 · Lamayuru monastery, Ladakh, at dusk
palettes.title: The cliff-side monastery as the lamps go on inside the prayer hall and the snow on the range above takes the last sun
palettes.source: Color from a Ladakhi Buddhist monastery at 3,500 m: earthen adobe exterior walls, the oxide-red of the prayer-hall band, marigold offerings, thangka cobalt, the rinse-white of a mani wall, juniper smoke rising into cold blue dusk.

swatches.name: Earthen wall, sun-baked adobe
swatches.note: The dominant exterior surface of every Ladakhi monastery building.

swatches.name: Snow-rinse white, mani wall
swatches.note: Annually rewhitewashed mani wall along the access path; reflects late light strongly.

swatches.name: Juniper smoke, cold-blue veil
swatches.note: Ritual juniper-bough smoke from the morning offering, still hanging at dusk.

swatches.name: Thangka cobalt
swatches.note: Mineral cobalt used in painted scroll backgrounds inside the assembly hall.

swatches.name: Prayer-hall red, oxide pigment
swatches.note: Iron-oxide red band painted around the prayer-hall facade.

swatches.name: Marigold gold, offering
swatches.note: Flowers heaped on the altar at the puja; brief saturated yellow inside an otherwise muted territory.

d.text: Adobe carries the territory at this hour; the warm dominant against the cold sky.

s.text: Mani-wall white, juniper smoke, thangka cobalt — the cooler register.

a.text: Prayer-hall red and marigold gold, where the ritual leaves its mark.

palettes.refuses: Saffron monk-robe orange as primary. Saffron is the Western shorthand for Buddhism; the actual monastery wall is earth, not robe.

volumes.title: Cities at Night
volumes.eyebrow: Field Notebook № 04 · Travel · Vol VI · Generative Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four cities, after dark, off the tourist hour.
preface: Cities at night are mostly sodium and shadow. The brand colours that work at noon — Buenos Aires's blue-and-white, Istanbul's tile, Hanoi's lacquer, Tbilisi's gold — recede; the working palette is the streetlamp and the asphalt.
Each of these four refuses the daytime cliché: the tango red, the Marmara turquoise, the jungle green, the cathedral gold. The dominant in every case is a working surface, not a brand.

palettes.kicker: 34° S · March · 22:00 · San Telmo, Buenos Aires, a Sunday after the antiques fair has closed
palettes.title: Cobblestone wet from the fountain wash, the sodium streetlights on, the parrillas just opening
palettes.source: Color from a Buenos Aires neighbourhood that has just emptied for the night: wet cobblestone, sodium streetlight cast on every facade, the fileteado sign-painter tradition on the bar windows, café-wall dulce-de-leche plaster.

swatches.name: Cobblestone wet, basalt set
swatches.note: The Sunday street wash leaves the granite block paving dark for an hour.

swatches.name: Café wall, dulce-de-leche cream
swatches.note: The standard 1940s parrilla facade colour — warm, never bright.

swatches.name: Mate green-grey, gourd
swatches.note: Worn-yerba colour on the corner table; the city's domestic drink, not a tourist signifier.

swatches.name: Asphalt sodium-spilled sky
swatches.note: The colour of the sky between streetlamps — not black, never blue.

swatches.name: Sodium streetlamp, low-pressure
swatches.note: Classic municipal lamp colour over the cobble; the night's warm wash.

swatches.name: Fileteado red, sign-painter
swatches.note: The deep oxide-red of the carrera-fileteado tradition on bar windows and grocer's signs.

d.text: Wet cobblestone is the entire ground plane after the fair closes.

s.text: Dulce-de-leche, mate, sodium-asphalt sky — the surfaces around the cobble.

a.text: Streetlamp warmth and fileteado red, in small reads only.

palettes.refuses: Tango black-and-red as primary. That palette is the dance hall and the brochure; this is the empty Sunday street outside it.

palettes.kicker: 41° N · November · 00:10 · Eminönü waterfront, Istanbul, last ferries in
palettes.title: The Galata bridge in light rain, with the simit carts still out and the Bosphorus working under fluorescent
palettes.source: Color from an Istanbul waterfront after midnight in autumn: Bosphorus diesel-water green, granite mosque shadow above the bridge, simit gold on the cart, the amber of çay in a tulip glass, the muted night sky over the Marmara.

swatches.name: Bosphorus diesel-water green
swatches.note: Late-autumn water along the seawall, churned by ferry traffic.

swatches.name: Granite mosque shadow
swatches.note: The Yeni Camii silhouette above the bridge, lit only from below.

swatches.name: Fluorescent ferry cabin
swatches.note: The slightly green-white of the city's working ferry interiors.

swatches.name: Stamboul night sky, sodium-haze
swatches.note: Light-polluted sky over Eminönü; never really black.

swatches.name: Simit sesame gold, cart-side
swatches.note: The vendor's stacked simit, lit by the cart's tungsten bulb.

swatches.name: Çay tulip-glass amber
swatches.note: Tea-brown read through a small glass on the seawall railing.

d.text: Bosphorus green-grey is the entire waterway and reflects up onto every wall.

s.text: Mosque granite, fluorescent ferry, night sky — the surrounding fabric.

a.text: Simit gold and çay amber — the night's only warm punctuation.

palettes.refuses: Postcard turquoise of the Marmara. The Bosphorus at midnight is diesel grey-green, not the noon-tour-boat blue.

palettes.kicker: 21° N · May · 02:00 · Hanoi Old Quarter, the hour after the pho stalls close
palettes.title: A single fluorescent strip in a shuttered shop, the ochre walls of the colonial block reading green under the light
palettes.source: Color from the deepest off-hour of the Old Quarter: French-colonial ochre stucco, single-tube fluorescent making the ochre read green, motorbike lacquer, plastic-stool red left out at the corner, lamplit banyan.

swatches.name: French-colonial ochre, wall
swatches.note: The dominant Old Quarter facade colour; the city's signature, even at 2 am.

swatches.name: Banyan leaf, lamplit
swatches.note: Ficus benghalensis on the corner, lit only by the shop's tube.

swatches.name: Phở broth steam, pale
swatches.note: Vapor still rising from a half-cleared stall at the corner.

swatches.name: Motorbike lacquer, deep maroon
swatches.note: The standard scooter paint job — deep, almost-black red.

swatches.name: Fluorescent tube, cold
swatches.note: Single overhead tube in the closed shop; the night's only direct light.

swatches.name: Plastic stool, sidewalk red
swatches.note: The ubiquitous sidewalk-dining stool, stacked at the corner.

d.text: French-colonial ochre is the city's wall; it carries the territory.

s.text: Banyan, phở steam, motorbike lacquer — the working night materials.

a.text: Fluorescent cold and plastic-stool red — the night's only saturated reads.

palettes.refuses: Lush travel-blog jungle green. Hanoi's Old Quarter is street and shutter, not jungle.

palettes.kicker: 41° N · October · 23:00 · Tbilisi viewed from the Mtatsminda funicular at the upper station
palettes.title: Soviet apartment blocks below, Old Town terracotta lit from beneath, the Mtkvari gorge already in shadow
palettes.source: Color from a Caucasian city viewed from above at night: Soviet apartment blocks grey by frequency, Old Town terracotta lit from below, the gorge of the Mtkvari in deep shadow, the sulfur-spring Abanotubani district faint-yellow.

swatches.name: Soviet apartment block, panel grey
swatches.note: The dominant frequency of any night view from Mtatsminda; thousands of pre-fab blocks.

swatches.name: Slate sky, Caucasus cool
swatches.note: October night over the city, no moon.

swatches.name: Mtkvari river gorge, deep
swatches.note: The river itself, unlit between the bridges.

swatches.name: Sulfur spring yellow, Abanotubani
swatches.note: The bath-house quarter dome-lights — pale yellow steam visible from the upper station.

swatches.name: Old Town terracotta, lit from below
swatches.note: The clay-tiled facades of Old Tbilisi, lit by floodlight at street level.

swatches.name: Candle-lit balcony, ochre warm
swatches.note: The single small light read from a wooden carved balcony — the territory's last warm note.

d.text: Block grey carries the view; everything is read against it.

s.text: Slate sky, gorge dark, sulfur-spring yellow — the surrounding fabric.

a.text: Old Town terracotta and balcony candlelight, in small bright reads.

palettes.refuses: Cathedral-gold saturated highlight. Tbilisi at night is brick and concrete; the gold-leaf-on-blue palette is a different country's postcard.

volumes.title: Markets & Material Culture
volumes.eyebrow: Field Notebook № 04 · Travel · Vol VII · Generative Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four markets, working hours, not the food-tour version.
preface: Markets are the densest single source of material culture in any city — and the most over-pictured. The strategy here is to find the dominant working colour, not the most photogenic stall. Counter wood. Vendor apron. Drying mat. Tile back-of-house.
Each palette names the pigment, the textile, or the cured material that anchors it. Pagoda gold, designer azulejo, rainbow ethnic-textile, Mediterranean cobalt — all refused in their place.

palettes.kicker: 16° N · March · 17:00 · Bogyoke Aung San Market, Yangon, last hour before closing
palettes.title: Longyi indigo on every vendor, lacquerware stacked, gold-leaf packets at the goldsmith counter, jade strung in baskets
palettes.source: Color from Yangon's main covered market in the hot-dry season: longyi indigo as the working dress code, lacquerware oxblood from Bagan tradition, gold-leaf at the goldsmith stall, raw jade from the northern hills, thanakha cream applied to faces, market lamp warm.

swatches.name: Longyi indigo, vendor cloth
swatches.note: The dominant moving colour of the market — every other vendor wears it.

swatches.name: Thanakha cream, applied to face
swatches.note: Sandalwood paste worn as sun-protection and cosmetic across the country.

swatches.name: Jade, raw stone
swatches.note: Unworked Burmese jadeite strung on cord at the stone counter.

swatches.name: Market lamp, tungsten warm
swatches.note: Bare-bulb illumination at the goldsmith and lacquer stalls.

swatches.name: Lacquerware oxblood, Bagan tradition
swatches.note: The deep red of yun-de lacquer bowls stacked at the craft stall.

swatches.name: Gold leaf, prayer-offering packet
swatches.note: Small folded squares sold by the goldsmith for temple offering.

d.text: Longyi indigo carries the market by sheer number of vendors.

s.text: Thanakha, jade, lamp warm — the working palette around the indigo.

a.text: Lacquer oxblood and gold leaf where the craft stalls cluster.

palettes.refuses: Pagoda gold as primary. Gold belongs to the temple; the market is indigo and lacquer first.

palettes.kicker: 41° N · April · 09:00 · La Boqueria, Barcelona, just past opening on a Tuesday
palettes.title: Full-bright fluorescent overhead, butchers up, the persimmon stand still being arranged
palettes.source: Color from a working European market the moment it opens for the day: butcher's tile, fluorescent shed white, cured Iberico ham, persimmon orange, brown paper cones, vendor aprons in marine navy.

swatches.name: Butcher's tile, glazed cream-grey
swatches.note: Floor and back-wall tile across every meat counter in the market.

swatches.name: Paper cone, kraft brown
swatches.note: Standard market-issue wrapping for olives, almonds, dried fruit.

swatches.name: Vendor apron, marine navy
swatches.note: The standard fishmonger and butcher apron across the stalls.

swatches.name: Fluorescent overhead, market shed white
swatches.note: The 1914 iron-and-glass roof plus modern fluorescents — a slightly green-white wash.

swatches.name: Iberico ham, cured deep
swatches.note: The dominant red in the market by hung-square-footage.

swatches.name: Persimmon orange, fruit stall
swatches.note: Stacked Sharon-fruit pyramid on the central island — the single most saturated colour visible.

d.text: Butcher's tile is the entire back wall of every counter.

s.text: Paper cones, vendor aprons, fluorescent shed white — the working surfaces.

a.text: Cured Iberico and persimmon orange, in small intense reads.

palettes.refuses: Mediterranean-postcard cobalt. La Boqueria is fluorescent-lit and tiled, not a Costa Brava shot.

palettes.kicker: 22° N · January · 11:00 · Sapa Sunday market, Lào Cai Province, cold mountain fog
palettes.title: Hmong textile and the working materials of the market: indigo, mountain mist, hanging maize, woven bamboo
palettes.source: Color from a Hmong textile market in winter fog: indigo hand-dyed and re-dyed, batik wax cream where the indigo is reserved, mountain mist on every surface, dry-corn yellow hung to roast, rhododendron pink gone over for the winter.

swatches.name: Hmong indigo, dyed and re-dyed
swatches.note: The deepest blue-black on the bolt-cloth; many passes through the dye pot.

swatches.name: Mountain mist white
swatches.note: The entire visual background of the market in winter morning.

swatches.name: Batik wax, undyed reserve
swatches.note: Cream of the wax-resist pattern where the indigo did not take.

swatches.name: Bamboo basket, woven yellow-green
swatches.note: Stacked carry-baskets at every textile stall.

swatches.name: Rhododendron, faded winter pink
swatches.note: The hill-slope flower out of season, dusty and pale.

swatches.name: Dry corn, hung-yellow
swatches.note: Cobs of maize hung above the meat stalls to dry-roast.

d.text: Hmong indigo carries the textile half of the market entirely.

s.text: Mountain mist, batik wax cream, bamboo basket — the cooler register.

a.text: Rhododendron pink and dry-corn yellow, small bright threads.

palettes.refuses: Rainbow-stripe ethnic-textile collage. Hmong work is mostly indigo with small bright threads; collapsing it into rainbow is the souvenir trade, not the maker.

palettes.kicker: 41° N · April · 10:00 · Bolhão Market, Porto, Saturday opening hour
palettes.title: Counter wood and roof glass, copper pots strung above the kitchen stalls, broken azulejo backsplash behind the fishmonger
palettes.source: Color from Porto's old central market: counter wood across every stall, market-roof glass grey, copper hardware, cured bacalhau on the rack, chipped azulejo backsplash (back-of-house, not designer), rooster-red lettering.

swatches.name: Counter wood, oiled hardwood
swatches.note: The dominant working surface — every stall counter, every chopping board.

swatches.name: Market roof glass-grey
swatches.note: The iron-and-glass ceiling overhead; reads as a flat overcast diffuser.

swatches.name: Cured bacalhau, dried cod
swatches.note: Salt-dried cod hung at the fishmonger; the dominant cream of the market.

swatches.name: Copper pot, market hardware
swatches.note: Strung-up pots and pans above the kitchen stalls; a regional hardware colour.

swatches.name: Broken azulejo, vendor backsplash
swatches.note: The market's tile is chipped and back-of-house — the working blue, not the designer's blue.

swatches.name: Rooster-lettering red, sign
swatches.note: Hand-painted vendor sign red — the colour of Galo de Barcelos lettering.

d.text: Counter wood is every stall, every chopping board, the entire working surface.

s.text: Roof glass-grey, cured cod, copper hardware — the materials around the wood.

a.text: Azulejo and lettering red, in chipped working amounts.

palettes.refuses: Pristine designer-restaurant azulejo. The market's tile is chipped and behind the counter; clean luxury-blue is the wrong register entirely.

volumes.title: Rail & Transit
volumes.eyebrow: Field Notebook № 04 · Travel · Vol VIII · Generative Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four trains, four hours not on the brochure.
preface: Train palettes are mostly engineered — the upholstery and the panel are spec'd by a transport authority, not by light. So the strategy is to find the engineering palette and then admit the one or two outside colours that survive the journey: the window, the platform, the conductor's uniform, the dining-car cloth.
Each of these refuses the easy outside-the-train postcard — the aurora, the Cycladic blue, the Outback turquoise, the spice quarter. The dominant is always something the railway chose.

palettes.kicker: 67° N · January · 03:00 · The Helsinki–Rovaniemi night train, somewhere past Oulu
palettes.title: The sleeper carriage at the deepest hour, with the platform sodium briefly reading orange across the snow
palettes.source: Color from a Finnish overnight sleeper in January: birch-panel cream interior, forest-green sleeper curtains, brushed aluminium fittings, the platform sodium glimpsed on snow during a station stop, dining-car navy upholstery.

swatches.name: Birch-panel cream, Finnish design
swatches.note: Standard VR overnight-carriage panelling; warm-cream against the snow outside.

swatches.name: Sleeper curtain, forest green
swatches.note: Heavy compartment curtain; the dominant rest-state surface when drawn.

swatches.name: Aluminium fitting, brushed
swatches.note: Door handles, ladder rails, sink fixtures — the carriage hardware.

swatches.name: Dining-car upholstery navy
swatches.note: Restaurant-car bench fabric, two carriages forward.

swatches.name: Platform sodium on snow
swatches.note: Three-second flash of station light during the Oulu stop.

swatches.name: Route map red, line marker
swatches.note: The single red line on the printed map in every compartment.

d.text: Birch panelling is every compartment wall and ceiling.

s.text: Curtain forest-green, aluminium, dining navy — the engineered surfaces.

a.text: Platform sodium and route-red, in tiny reads.

palettes.refuses: Aurora green as carriage colour. Keep the aurora to the window; let the carriage be Finnish modernism.

palettes.kicker: 37° N · October · 22:00 · Athens–Crete overnight ferry, on the open deck above the couchette saloon
palettes.title: Couchette tan vinyl below, fluorescent in the saloon, the life-ring still bright against the rust hull at the waterline
palettes.source: Color from a Greek inter-island working ferry: vinyl couchette tan, a deckhand's navy uniform, life-ring international orange, ferry-hull rust at the waterline, ouzo glass cloudy, the green-white of a lounge fluorescent.

swatches.name: Vinyl couchette tan, deck seat
swatches.note: 1980s passenger-couchette vinyl; the dominant interior surface below decks.

swatches.name: Ouzo glass cloudy
swatches.note: Anise spirit in water on the deck table; the only off-white reading.

swatches.name: Deckhand uniform navy
swatches.note: Crew-issue navy work shirt, sun-faded.

swatches.name: Lounge fluorescent, green-white
swatches.note: The single overhead in the passenger saloon, a familiar register from Vol I's ferry.

swatches.name: Life-ring, international orange
swatches.note: Mounted on every bulkhead by regulation; the most saturated colour aboard.

swatches.name: Ferry-hull rust, splash zone
swatches.note: Iron-oxide bloom along the hull at the waterline, visible from the open deck.

d.text: Vinyl couchette tan is every seat, headrest, and bulkhead below decks.

s.text: Ouzo cloudy, deckhand navy, saloon fluorescent — the working surroundings.

a.text: Life-ring orange and hull rust, by regulation and weather.

palettes.refuses: Cycladic white-and-blue. That palette is for the deck-chair brochure; this is the below-decks passage, not the daytime postcard.

palettes.kicker: 31° S · September · 14:00 · The Indian Pacific between Cook and Adelaide, mid-Nullarbor
palettes.title: Carriage cream against red soil to the horizon, with the saltbush only ever appearing in the middle distance
palettes.source: Color from an Australian transcontinental rail crossing: 1970s carriage-laminate cream as the interior dominant, the Nullarbor's red soil and gibber plain through the window, saltbush blue-grey in the middle distance, a parched pale sky, a starched dining-car tablecloth.

swatches.name: Carriage cream, 1970s laminate
swatches.note: Original Indian Pacific interior surface; the dominant interior read.

swatches.name: Pale Outback sky, parched
swatches.note: Nullarbor sky in September; never the deep blue of an inland holiday brochure.

swatches.name: Saltbush blue-grey, middle distance
swatches.note: Atriplex on the salt-pan plain; the only consistent green in the territory.

swatches.name: Dining tablecloth, starched white
swatches.note: Service cloth on the Restaurant Car table at lunch.

swatches.name: Nullarbor red soil
swatches.note: Iron-oxide topsoil; the dominant landscape colour the train moves through.

swatches.name: Gibber plain, rust pebble
swatches.note: Wind-polished surface stones across the long stretches between sidings.

d.text: Carriage cream — the interior carries the dominant for everyone reading or sleeping.

s.text: Pale sky, saltbush, tablecloth — the cooler surrounds.

a.text: Nullarbor red and gibber rust — outside the window, in pulses.

palettes.refuses: Tropical-Australia turquoise. The Nullarbor sees no sea; the only blue is the dry sky.

palettes.kicker: 33° N · April · 10:30 · ONCF Al Boraq high-speed train, Tangier-bound from Casablanca
palettes.title: Modernist engineering palette against the sahel, with the Atlas snow still on the high horizon and almond orchards in late bloom
palettes.source: Color from Morocco's high-speed rail in spring: train silver livery, the sahel ochre of the low hills, distant Atlas snow, the cobalt station tile at Casa Voyageurs, almond-blossom pink in the orchards, oxidised overhead-line copper.

swatches.name: High-speed train silver, livery
swatches.note: Al Boraq's external livery — modernist silver, not heritage colour.

swatches.name: Sahel ochre, low hills
swatches.note: Coastal-plain soil and stubble fields between Kenitra and Tangier.

swatches.name: Atlas snow distant, peak
swatches.note: Mid-Atlas snowcap on the far horizon at this time of year.

swatches.name: Overhead-line copper, oxidised
swatches.note: Catenary copper that the train slides under at 320 km/h.

swatches.name: Station tile blue, Casa Voyageurs
swatches.note: Cobalt-on-tin glazed tile in the modern station's circulation halls.

swatches.name: Almond blossom, late-spring orchard
swatches.note: Brief mid-April bloom in the orchards visible from the window.

d.text: Train silver carries every interior surface and every reflection.

s.text: Sahel ochre, Atlas snow, oxidised copper — the moving landscape.

a.text: Station tile and almond blossom — small saturated reads at platforms and orchards.

palettes.refuses: Brochure-tourist saffron orange. Al Boraq is engineering modernism; the spice-quarter palette belongs to Vol I.

volumes.title: Sacred & Ritual Spaces
volumes.eyebrow: Field Notebook № 04 · Travel · Vol IX · Generative Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from rooms made for ritual, at the hour the ritual happens.
preface: Sacred spaces are designed for one moment: midnight, dawn, dusk, the hour the procession arrives. Sampling them outside that hour gives a museum colour; sampling them during it gives a working colour. Every palette here is the used version of the space.
Each refuses an outsider's signifier — cathedral cool blue, candy cherry-blossom pink, orientalist lavender, Halloween orange-and-black. The dominant is always the surface the rite is performed on.

palettes.kicker: 37° N · May · 00:00 · A Patmos Greek Orthodox church, Easter Saturday at midnight
palettes.title: The moment the priest emerges with the candle and the congregation lights from him outward
palettes.source: Color from Anastasis — the Orthodox Easter resurrection liturgy — on a small island church: candle-wax warm-white, gold-leaf icons, frankincense smoke, cold marble underfoot, sun-faded monk's habit, dyed red eggs on the table at the back.

swatches.name: Candle-wax warm-white, congregation
swatches.note: Hundreds of beeswax candles passed person-to-person at midnight; the night's dominant light.

swatches.name: Marble floor, cold grey
swatches.note: Greek island marble underfoot — cooler than the candle-warmed air above.

swatches.name: Incense smoke, frankincense
swatches.note: Liturgical incense from the censer; the atmosphere everyone reads through.

swatches.name: Monk's habit, sun-faded black
swatches.note: Cassock fabric that has been worn outdoors enough to lose its true black.

swatches.name: Gold-leaf icon, iconostasis
swatches.note: Hammered gold backing on the icons above the royal doors.

swatches.name: Red dyed Easter egg, table
swatches.note: Eggs dyed in onion-skin red for the Easter table at the back of the nave.

d.text: Candle warm-white is the entire light source after midnight.

s.text: Marble, incense smoke, monk's habit — the spatial container.

a.text: Gold-leaf icons and red eggs, the two saturated reads of the night.

palettes.refuses: Saturated cathedral-blue. Orthodox interiors are warm; the cool blue is Catholic, not Aegean island night.

palettes.kicker: 34° N · May · 04:30 · The corridor of torii at Fushimi Inari before opening hour
palettes.title: The first hour of the morning, when the lower paths are empty and the dew is still on the vermillion
palettes.source: Color from the famous Kyoto Inari shrine just before dawn: fresh vermillion lacquer on the inner torii, weathered vermillion on the older outer ones, cryptomeria bark, gravel-path moss, the grey stone of kitsune fox statues, the cool blue of pre-dawn sky through the canopy.

swatches.name: Vermillion lacquer, fresh torii
swatches.note: The specific lead-mercury shu vermillion of recently re-painted torii.

swatches.name: Cryptomeria bark, forest pillar
swatches.note: Standing trunk of Cryptomeria japonica between every gate cluster.

swatches.name: Moss, gravel path
swatches.note: Compacted moss on the path edge, kept damp by the shaded canopy.

swatches.name: Stone fox grey, kitsune statue
swatches.note: Granite or stone kitsune guardian — the cool counterweight to the vermillion.

swatches.name: Weathered vermillion, outer torii
swatches.note: Older outer-route gates, sun-bleached over years toward terracotta.

swatches.name: Pre-dawn blue, sky through canopy
swatches.note: Narrow strip of sky visible above the topmost torii at 04:30.

d.text: Vermillion is the territory; every other reading sits inside it.

s.text: Cryptomeria bark, moss, stone fox grey — the forest around the gates.

a.text: Weathered torii and pre-dawn blue, in small reads only.

palettes.refuses: Candy cherry-blossom pink. The torii is a specific mineral pigment; collapsing it into sakura pink misnames the chemistry.

palettes.kicker: 26° N · June · 18:30 · The shrine of Lal Shahbaz Qalandar, Sehwan, at the evening dhamaal
palettes.title: Drum, marigold, mirror-mosaic, white shrouds — the moment the qawwali starts and the courtyard fills
palettes.source: Color from a major Sufi dargah at the height of devotion: shrine-tile emerald on every interior surface, marigold garland gold, mirror-mosaic silver, the qawwali drum-leather brown, the white kafan cloth of devotees, the desert sun reading red through dust.

swatches.name: Shrine-tile emerald
swatches.note: Glazed Multan/Hala-tradition tile across the dome interior and walls.

swatches.name: White kafan cloth, devotee
swatches.note: Ritual white worn by the gathered pilgrims; the dominant moving colour.

swatches.name: Mirror-mosaic silver, dome interior
swatches.note: Sheesh-work mirror fragments inlaid into the upper dome.

swatches.name: Qawwali drum brown, leather
swatches.note: Tabla and dholak skins under the musicians' courtyard.

swatches.name: Marigold garland gold
swatches.note: Gainda flowers strung in lengths across the threshold; renewed each evening.

swatches.name: Dust-haze red sun
swatches.note: The sun setting through Sindh-summer dust outside the courtyard wall.

d.text: Shrine emerald reads from every wall and dome interior.

s.text: Kafan white, mirror silver, drum-leather — the working ritual textures.

a.text: Marigold gold and red-dust sun, at threshold and horizon.

palettes.refuses: Pastel orientalist lavender. The shrine is saturated, hot, and noisy; the soft mystical palette is an outsider's project.

palettes.kicker: 17° N · November · 22:00 · An Oaxacan village cemetery on the first night of Día de los Muertos
palettes.title: Whitewashed tombs covered in marigold, candles lit, papel picado overhead, copal smoke heavy in the still air
palettes.source: Color from the most photographed ritual in the Americas, sampled in the working hour rather than the brochure hour: tomb whitewash, cempasúchil marigold gold, papel picado magenta, copal smoke blue, candle wax warm, fresh-dug earth.

swatches.name: Tomb whitewash, cal
swatches.note: Annually rewhitewashed cement tomb surface across the cemetery.

swatches.name: Cempasúchil marigold gold
swatches.note: Tagetes erecta petals laid in trails to guide the dead.

swatches.name: Copal smoke, blue-haze
swatches.note: Resin incense burned at every tomb; the air's actual texture this night.

swatches.name: Fresh-dug earth
swatches.note: Recently turned grave soil at the foot of each visited tomb.

swatches.name: Papel picado, magenta
swatches.note: Cut paper banner strung between tombs; the night's most saturated colour.

swatches.name: Candle wax, warm flicker
swatches.note: Dozens of beeswax tapers per tomb; the literal local light source.

d.text: Tomb whitewash carries the entire cemetery surface.

s.text: Marigold, copal smoke, fresh earth — the night's working materials.

a.text: Papel picado magenta and candle wax warm — saturated punctuation.

palettes.refuses: Halloween black-and-orange. Día de los Muertos and Halloween share a date and nothing else; the accent here is magenta, not corner-store orange.

volumes.title: Deserts & Arid Territories
volumes.eyebrow: Field Notebook № 04 · Travel · Vol X · Generative Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four deserts, four very different reasons nothing grows.
preface: Deserts get treated as one category; they are not. The Empty Quarter is sand and sky, Wadi Rum is sandstone and basalt, the Skeleton Coast is fog and bone, the Great Salt Lake is hypersaline brine. Each makes a different palette refusal mandatory.
Specifically: oil-money gold, Petra-rose monochrome, safari-poster orange, lagoon-turquoise. Each is the wrong colour for its territory and is named in the refuses line.

palettes.kicker: 20° N · January · 06:30 · Rub' al Khali at first light, near the Saudi-Omani border
palettes.title: Sand dunes the size of small mountains, with the east face already pink and the west face still in shadow
palettes.source: Color from the Empty Quarter at the first hour of light: dune-crest pink on the sunrise faces, dune-shadow violet on the west, undyed camel hair, Bedouin black-goat-hair tent, low-humidity thin sky, mineral salt-crust at the sabkha.

swatches.name: Dune-crest pink, sunrise face
swatches.note: Iron-oxide-coated quartz sand catching the first sun.

swatches.name: Mineral salt crust, sabkha
swatches.note: Evaporite crust in inter-dune lows; the second great pale surface.

swatches.name: Thin pale sky, low humidity
swatches.note: Cloudless sky at 23° N in January; never deep, low-humidity pale.

swatches.name: Camel hair, undyed cream
swatches.note: The literal animal of the territory and the woven fibre of its saddles.

swatches.name: Dune-shadow violet, west face
swatches.note: The cool shadow side of any large dune at this hour.

swatches.name: Bedouin black tent, goat hair
swatches.note: Hand-woven bayt al-sha'r — the dark fabric of every encampment.

d.text: Sunrise dune pink — the entire visible east face at this hour.

s.text: Salt crust, thin sky, camel hair — the wider pale envelope.

a.text: Shadow violet and black tent — the only dark reads in the territory.

palettes.refuses: Oil-rich-Gulf gold-and-glass. The desert at dawn refuses the corporate gold of the cities behind it.

palettes.kicker: 30° N · March · 16:00 · Wadi Rum, the Jebel Khazali wall in late afternoon
palettes.title: Sandstone red the size of cathedrals and basalt scree at the base, with the air visibly layered by haze
palettes.source: Color from Wadi Rum in spring: Nubian sandstone red on the high cliffs, basalt black in the scree at the base, wind-polished pale sand on the wadi floor, cardamom-tea brown, sun-cracked saddle leather, the distant haze that the desert builds in layers.

swatches.name: Nubian sandstone red, cliff
swatches.note: The high cliff walls of Jebel Khazali and the surrounding jebels.

swatches.name: Wind-polished sand, wadi floor
swatches.note: Pale fine sand swept smooth between rock walls.

swatches.name: Distant haze, layered air
swatches.note: Atmospheric haze that stacks visibly in the wadi by mid-afternoon.

swatches.name: Saddle leather, sun-cracked
swatches.note: Bedouin camel saddle, the dominant worked material in camp.

swatches.name: Basalt black, scree
swatches.note: Volcanic boulders fallen to the cliff base; small reads but defining contrast.

swatches.name: Cardamom tea brown
swatches.note: Bedouin afternoon tea — material culture of the encampment.

d.text: Nubian sandstone is the entire vertical of the territory.

s.text: Wadi-floor sand, distant haze, saddle leather — the horizontal and the worked.

a.text: Basalt scree and tea, in small reads at base and camp.

palettes.refuses: Petra-rose monochrome. Petra is a specific sandstone two hundred kilometres north; Wadi Rum is harder, redder, and has black basalt.

palettes.kicker: 20° S · July · 10:00 · The Skeleton Coast in dense Atlantic fog, near Cape Cross
palettes.title: Where the fog comes off the cold current and the only colour for kilometres is rust on a wrecked hull
palettes.source: Color from a Namibian shore that no postcard market wants: fog-into-dune ochre reading in mixed light, seal-bone pale on the beach, the Atlantic's cold green, oryx coat grey, lichen rust on a shipwreck and the oxidised steel of the wreck itself.

swatches.name: Fog-into-dune ochre
swatches.note: The dunes seen through Benguela fog — a desaturated, mixed reading.

swatches.name: Oryx grey, antelope coat
swatches.note: The single large mammal of the territory.

swatches.name: Seal-bone pale, beach litter
swatches.note: Bleached cape-fur-seal bone scattered along the strandline.

swatches.name: Atlantic cold green, current
swatches.note: The Benguela cold-current sea, never warm-blue here.

swatches.name: Lichen rust, on shipwreck
swatches.note: Orange lichen growing on wrecked iron plating.

swatches.name: Shipwreck steel, oxidised
swatches.note: The dark rust of an iron hull that has been here decades.

d.text: Fog-into-dune ochre carries the entire visible inland.

s.text: Oryx grey, seal-bone, Atlantic cold green — the working surrounds.

a.text: Lichen rust and oxidised steel only where the wrecks are.

palettes.refuses: African safari-poster orange. The Skeleton Coast is the un-safari — cold, fogged, lethal, grey.

palettes.kicker: 41° N · July · 20:30 · The Great Salt Lake at sunset, near Antelope Island causeway
palettes.title: Hypersaline pink water against mineral-white salt and the Wasatch silhouette behind
palettes.source: Color from one of the planet's largest hypersaline bodies in midsummer: pink algae brine from Dunaliella bloom, mineral salt crust on the shoreline, sage on the hillside, gull-white, Wasatch silhouette, the orange sky-reflection on the water.

swatches.name: Mineral salt crust
swatches.note: Halite crust where the lake has receded; reads pure white from the causeway.

swatches.name: Gull-white, brackish bird
swatches.note: Larus californicus feeding on the brine flies along the shore.

swatches.name: Sage hillside, green-grey
swatches.note: Artemisia tridentata on the slope above the causeway.

swatches.name: Mountain silhouette, Wasatch
swatches.note: The range behind the lake at sunset, taking the warm reflected light from the west.

swatches.name: Pink algae brine, Dunaliella bloom
swatches.note: The lake's specific pink water — biological, not optical illusion.

swatches.name: Sunset orange, sky reflection
swatches.note: Late-July sun reflected through the haze of a desert summer evening.

d.text: Salt crust is the dominant mineral surface of the foreshore.

s.text: Gull white, sage hillside, Wasatch silhouette — the surrounding fabric.

a.text: Algae pink and sunset orange — the two warm reads of the hour.

palettes.refuses: Turquoise-lagoon palette. The lake is hypersaline and pink, not Caribbean blue.

volumes.title: Forests & Wilderness
volumes.eyebrow: Field Notebook № 04 · Travel · Vol XI · Generative Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four forests, no fairy tales, just the ground cover and the canopy.
preface: Forest palettes are almost always rendered as green-on-green; in working forests, green is rarely the dominant. The Tongass is gloom; the Schwarzwald is canopy-dark; the Atchafalaya is grey; the Kamchatkan taiga is overcast.
Each refuses an external marketing colour — calendar teal, Grimm-tourism red, neon-bayou magenta, Kamchatka-poster glacier-teal. The dominant is what you actually walk on or stand under.

palettes.kicker: 57° N · September · 14:00 · Tongass National Forest, old-growth Sitka spruce in steady rain
palettes.title: Under the canopy in autumn rain, with the salmon stream just visible through the moss and the bark soaked black
palettes.source: Color from the world's largest temperate rainforest in active rain: gloom-under-canopy grey-green, Sitka-spruce bark wet, sphagnum and salmonberry below, the salmon-run flesh tone of the spawning fish, glacial-melt creek pale.

swatches.name: Gloom under canopy, indirect light
swatches.note: The dominant ambient at midday under old-growth canopy in rain.

swatches.name: Sitka spruce bark, wet
swatches.note: Picea sitchensis trunk, soaked through after hours of rain.

swatches.name: Sphagnum moss, green-yellow
swatches.note: Continuous moss carpet across roots, stumps, and forest floor.

swatches.name: Glacier-melt creek, mineral grey-green
swatches.note: Outwash stream colour where it cuts through the forest.

swatches.name: Salmonberry red, fruit
swatches.note: Rubus spectabilis berry on the brush at trail-side.

swatches.name: Salmon flesh, sockeye
swatches.note: Oncorhynchus nerka in the spawning channel, visible through the surface.

d.text: Canopy gloom — the dominant ambient most of the year.

s.text: Wet bark, sphagnum, glacier creek — the actual textures under foot and hand.

a.text: Salmonberry and salmon flesh, the only saturated reds of the forest.

palettes.refuses: Bright travel-magazine glacier-teal. The Tongass is a temperate rainforest, not a glacier-pool calendar shot.

palettes.kicker: 48° N · November · 11:30 · The Schwarzwald between St. Märgen and Hinterzarten, low cloud through the spruce
palettes.title: Beech leaves down on the path, spruce canopy dark above, the cottage half-timber across the small stream
palettes.source: Color from a German upland forest at the end of autumn: spruce canopy dark, beech-leaf rust on the path, fog between the trunks, half-timbered cottage wood, mineral-clear stream, the chanterelle yellow still pushing up through the duff.

swatches.name: Spruce canopy dark
swatches.note: Picea abies canopy at this latitude in November.

swatches.name: Half-timbered cottage, oiled hardwood
swatches.note: Oiled cladding on the few cottages visible from the path.

swatches.name: Stream water, mineral-clear
swatches.note: Cold-flow stream cutting between the spruce stands.

swatches.name: Tannenbaum mist, fog between trunks
swatches.note: Low-altitude cloud sitting in the woodland; the visibility of the day.

swatches.name: Beech leaf rust, fallen
swatches.note: Fagus sylvatica leaf cover on the forest path.

swatches.name: Chanterelle yellow, mushroom
swatches.note: Cantharellus cibarius still pushing up through the autumn duff.

d.text: Spruce canopy is the whole vertical of the territory.

s.text: Cottage wood, stream water, mist — the working surrounds.

a.text: Beech leaf and chanterelle, on the ground only.

palettes.refuses: Grimm-fairy-tale red. The forest is not Disney; it is wet, dark, fungal, and largely without strong colour.

palettes.kicker: 30° N · May · 06:00 · Atchafalaya basin cypress slough, sunrise from a flat-bottom boat
palettes.title: Cypress knees, Spanish moss, swamp water tannin-black, the egret white standing impossibly bright against the lot
palettes.source: Color from a Louisiana cypress slough at dawn: cypress-knee grey, Spanish moss silver, tannin-black swamp water, alligator-mud brown, the brilliant white of a wading egret, dawn pink in the humidity haze.

swatches.name: Cypress-knee grey, knot
swatches.note: Taxodium distichum root knees poking above the water surface.

swatches.name: Spanish moss, silver drape
swatches.note: Tillandsia usneoides hanging in every available limb.

swatches.name: Alligator-mud brown
swatches.note: Bank silt where the alligators come out to bask.

swatches.name: Dawn pink, humidity-haze
